21	## `hjt` — SOLVED, compiles axiom-clean (verbatim proof below)
22	
23	**Status: DONE.** The proof below was compiled in this iteration via `lean_run_code` against
24	`import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.OpenImmersionPushforward` +
25	`import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.AbsoluteCohomology`. `#print axioms q2` returns exactly
26	`[propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound]` — axiom-clean.
27	
28	```lean
29	noncomputable def q2 {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (U : Scheme.{u}) (φ : U ≅ Spec R) (V : U.Opens) :
30	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardEquivOfIso φ).functor.obj (jShriekOU V)
31	      ≅ jShriekOU (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V) := by
32	  set Φ := Scheme.Modules.pushforwardEquivOfIso φ
33	  set A := jShriekOU V
34	  have e1 : coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op (Φ.functor.obj A))
35	      ≅ Φ.inverse ⋙ coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op A) :=
36	    (Φ.toAdjunction.compCoyonedaIso).app (Opposite.op A)
37	  have cV : coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op A)
38	      ≅ AlgebraicGeometry.sectionsFunctor V ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat :=
39	    (Functor.isoWhiskerRight (sectionsFunctorCorepIso V) (forget AddCommGrpCat)).symm
40	  have cV' : AlgebraicGeometry.sectionsFunctor (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V) ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat
41	      ≅ coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op (jShriekOU (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V))) :=
42	    Functor.isoWhiskerRight (sectionsFunctorCorepIso (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V)) (forget AddCommGrpCat)
43	  have big : coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op (Φ.functor.obj A))
44	      ≅ coyoneda.obj (Opposite.op (jShriekOU (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V))) :=
45	    e1 ≪≫ Functor.isoWhiskerLeft Φ.inverse cV ≪≫
46	      (eqToIso (by rfl : Φ.inverse ⋙ (AlgebraicGeometry.sectionsFunctor V ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat)
47	        = AlgebraicGeometry.sectionsFunctor (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V) ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat)) ≪≫ cV'
48	  exact (Coyoneda.fullyFaithful.preimageIso big).symm.unop
49	```

51	**Open context required** for this to elaborate as-is:
52	`open CategoryTheory Limits AlgebraicGeometry Scheme.Modules Opposite`.

54	### Why it works (the load-bearing facts, all verified)
55	1. **`preadditiveCoyoneda.obj X ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat = coyoneda.obj X` is `rfl`.** So the
56	   project's `sectionsFunctorCorepIso V : sectionsFunctor V ≅ preadditiveCoyoneda.obj (op (jShriekOU V))`
57	   (already built in `OpenImmersionPushforward.lean:160`) whiskered by `forget AddCommGrpCat` gives an
58	   iso `coyoneda.obj (op (jShriekOU V)) ≅ sectionsFunctor V ⋙ forget` — the corepresentability of the
59	   *Type-valued* sections functor by `jShriekOU V`.
60	2. **`Scheme.Modules.pushforward f ⋙ sectionsFunctor V = sectionsFunctor (f ⁻¹ᵁ V)` is `rfl`**
61	   (sections of a pushforward are, definitionally, the sections over the preimage open —
62	   `pushforward_obj_obj` is `rfl`). This is why the `eqToIso (by rfl : …)` in `big` typechecks:
63	   `Φ.inverse = pushforward φ.inv`, so `Φ.inverse ⋙ sectionsFunctor V = sectionsFunctor (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V)`
64	   on the nose, hence also after whiskering with `forget`.
65	3. **`Adjunction.compCoyonedaIso`** is the Mathlib equivalence-coyoneda iso:
66	   `(Φ.toAdjunction.compCoyonedaIso).app (op A) : coyoneda.obj (op (Φ.functor.obj A)) ≅ Φ.inverse ⋙ coyoneda.obj (op A)`.
67	   It packages "a representable transported across an equivalence is corepresented on the other side
68	   by the inverse-image object."
69	4. **`Coyoneda.fullyFaithful : (coyoneda (C := …)).FullyFaithful`** reflects isos:
70	   `Coyoneda.fullyFaithful.preimageIso big : op (Φ.functor.obj A) ≅ op (jShriekOU (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ V))`,
71	   then `.symm.unop` lands the desired iso in the original (un-opped) direction.

77	(`Adjunction.compCoyonedaIso`, `Coyoneda.fullyFaithful`, `Functor.isoWhiskerLeft/Right`) plus the
78	project's already-built `sectionsFunctorCorepIso`. The only "geometry" is the two `rfl`s above. No

84	## `hqc` — minimal residual: "pushforward along a scheme iso preserves quasi-coherence" (Mathlib GAP)
85	
86	**Verdict: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.** There is no Mathlib lemma "pushforward (or pullback) of a
87	quasi-coherent module along a morphism preserves quasi-coherence" — the entire AG layer instantiates
88	`IsQuasicoherent` only for `tilde` (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Tilde.lean` is the *sole* AG
89	file mentioning `IsQuasicoherent`). Confirmed gaps this iteration:
90	- `infer_instance` for `(Φ.functor.obj H).IsQuasicoherent` from `[H.IsQuasicoherent]` — FAILS.
91	- No `SheafOfModules.pushforward`/`pullback` + `IsQuasicoherent` lemma in Mathlib (loogle empty).
92	
93	### Why no shortcut exists
94	`IsQuasicoherent M` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/Quasicoherent.lean:249`) is
95	`Nonempty (QuasicoherentData M)`, where `QuasicoherentData` (line 201) is a **local** datum:
96	a cover `X : I → C` of `⊤` in the *site* `C = Opens U` plus a `Presentation` of `M.over (X i)` for
97	each `i`. Two facts kill every "easy" route:
98	- **No global presentation of `H`.** `H` is qcoh on the *abstract affine* `U`, so it carries only
99	  *local* presentations. `Presentation.map` (line 178: transports a **global** `Presentation` across
100	  any colimit-preserving `F` with `η : F.obj (unit) ≅ unit`) is therefore inapplicable to `H`
101	  directly — there is no `H.Presentation` to feed it. Mathlib has `presentationTilde`
102	  (`Tilde.lean:376`, a global presentation of `tilde M` on `Spec R`) but only on the *target*,
103	  which is circular (it needs `Φ.functor.obj H ≅ tilde M`, i.e. qcoh, already).
104	- **`QuasicoherentData.ofIsIso` / iso-closure is same-site only.** `isQuasicoherent` IS closed under
105	  isomorphism — `SheafOfModules.instIsClosedUnderIsomorphismsIsQuasicoherent` (Quasicoherent.lean:330),
106	  used in the project as `(SheafOfModules.isQuasicoherent.{u} (Spec R).ringCatSheaf).prop_of_iso`
107	  (`QcohTildeSections.lean:108`). But this transports qcoh only **within one fixed site**
108	  `(Spec R).Modules`; it cannot bridge `U.Modules ⟶ (Spec R).Modules`, which is where `Φ` lives.
109	
110	So `hqc` genuinely requires moving `H`'s **local** qcoh data across the equivalence, and a functor
111	transport of a *local* condition needs the functor to commute with **restriction to opens** (the
112	over-site) — exactly the content that `QuasicoherentData.bind` (Quasicoherent.lean:360) internalises
113	via `pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence` + `Over.iteratedSliceEquiv`.
114

121	    (H : X.Modules) [H.IsQuasicoherent] :
122	    ((Scheme.Modules.pushforwardEquivOfIso φ).functor.obj H).IsQuasicoherent
123	```

127	1. `obtain ⟨q⟩ := (IsQuasicoherent.nonempty_quasicoherentData (M := H))` — a cover
128	   `q.X : q.I → Opens X` of `⊤` with `q.presentation i : (H.over (q.X i)).Presentation`.
129	2. Apply `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent.of_coversTop` (Quasicoherent.lean:377) with the **image
130	   cover** `fun i => φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ (q.X i) : q.I → Opens Y`.
131	   - `CoversTop` of the image cover: transport `q.coversTop` along the homeomorphism `φ.inv.base`
132	     (`φ` is an iso of schemes ⇒ `φ.inv.base` is a homeomorphism). Residual sub-fact **R3** (cheap).
133	   - **Sidestep the `of_coversTop` timeout** (directive note): supply each per-`i`
134	     `(((Φ.functor.obj H)).over (φ.inv ⁻¹ᵁ (q.X i))).IsQuasicoherent` as an explicit `haveI`

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso commutes with the free functor]
  \label{lem:pushforward_commutes_free}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_commutes_free}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and write
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi : X.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq Y.\mathrm{Modules}\)
  for the induced equivalence of module categories, with \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) the corresponding
  pushforward on presheaves of modules along the homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\). Then for every
  presheaf of modules \(P\) on \(X\) there is a natural isomorphism
  \[
    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(\operatorname{free} P\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{free}\bigl(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} P\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor of Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou}
  The free-module functor is the left adjoint of the forgetful functor from presheaves of modules to
  presheaves of abelian groups, and pushforward along the isomorphism \(\varphi\) is itself an
  equivalence that commutes with the forgetful functors --- it relabels opens along the underlying
  homeomorphism and leaves the underlying abelian-group data untouched. A left adjoint composed with an
  equivalence is again a left adjoint, now to the conjugate forgetful functor; the mate of the identity
  comparison between the two forgetful functors is the asserted natural isomorphism
  \(\Phi(\operatorname{free} P) \cong \operatorname{free}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} P)\), canonical by
  uniqueness of left adjoints.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso commutes with sheafification]
  \label{lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_commutes_sheafify}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
  With \(\varphi : X \cong Y\), \(\Phi\) and \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) as above, for every presheaf of
  modules \(Q\) on \(X\) there is a natural isomorphism
  \[
    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(\operatorname{sheafification} Q\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} Q\bigr).
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
  The homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of sites between the opens of \(X\) and the
  opens of \(Y\), under which covering sieves correspond. Hence the two sheafification adjunctions
  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}) correspond across
  the equivalence: both \(\Phi \circ \operatorname{sheafification}\) and
  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \circ \Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) are left adjoint to the common composite of
  the inclusion \(\iota\) with the presheaf transport \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}^{-1}\), so they agree up to
  the canonical natural isomorphism of left adjoints.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[The representable transports across the homeomorphism]
  \label{lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  For a scheme isomorphism \(\varphi : X \cong Y\), whose underlying homeomorphism induces the
  order-isomorphism of frames \(\operatorname{Opens} X \cong \operatorname{Opens} Y\), and any open
  \(V \subseteq X\), the presheaf pushforward of the representable presheaf carries it to the
  representable on the image open:
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\, V\bigr) \;\cong\; \mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \,{''}\, V),
  \]
  where \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \,{''}\, V\) is the image open of \(V\) in \(Y\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The presheaf pushforward \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) acts on opens by the inverse-image order-isomorphism
  \(W \mapsto \varphi^{-1}(W)\) of the homeomorphism. Hence
  \((\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\,\mathbf{y}\,V)(W) = (\mathbf{y}\,V)(\varphi^{-1} W)\) is inhabited exactly when
  \(\varphi^{-1} W \subseteq V\), i.e.\ when \(W \subseteq \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\); this is
  precisely \((\mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V))(W)\). The bijection is natural in \(W\)
  because the open-image map is monotone and inverts the restriction order, so the two representables
  coincide as presheaves.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[A scheme isomorphism carries the corepresenting object along]
  \label{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_transport_along_iso}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:pushforward_commutes_free, lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify,
    lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes, \(V \subseteq X\) an open, and
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi : X.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq Y.\mathrm{Modules}\)
  the induced equivalence. Then the pushforward carries the corepresenting object of
  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou} along the isomorphism:
  \[
    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V},
  \]
  where \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\) is the image open of \(V\) in \(Y\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:pushforward_commutes_free, lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify,
    lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}
  Unfold \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V = \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V))\)
  (Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}) and chase the three commutations. Pushforward commutes with
  sheafification (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_sheafify}):
  \(\Phi(\operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V))) \cong
  \operatorname{sheafification}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)))\). It commutes
  with the free functor (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_free}):
  \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)) \cong
  \operatorname{free}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\mathbf{y}\,V))\). And the representable transports to the
  representable on the image open (Lemma~\ref{lem:yoneda_transport_along_homeo}):
  \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}(\mathbf{y}\,V) \cong \mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V)\). Composing the
  three gives
  \(\Phi(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong
  \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V))) =
  j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Coyoneda transported across an equivalence]
  \label{lem:compCoyonedaIso_mathlib}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.compCoyonedaIso}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  For an adjunction \(F \dashv G\) between categories, the covariant representable functor corepresented
  by \(F(A)\) is naturally isomorphic to the composite of \(G\) with the representable corepresented by
  \(A\):
  \[
    \operatorname{coyoneda}\bigl(\mathrm{op}(F(A))\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    G \circ \operatorname{coyoneda}\bigl(\mathrm{op}\, A\bigr).
  \]
  Applied to the adjunction underlying an equivalence \(\Phi\), it packages ``a corepresentable
  transported across an equivalence is corepresented on the other side by the inverse-image object.''
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Coyoneda is fully faithful]
  \label{lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Coyoneda.fullyFaithful}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  The covariant Yoneda embedding \(\operatorname{coyoneda} : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
  (\mathcal{C} \to \mathrm{Type})\) is fully faithful; in particular it reflects isomorphisms, so a
  natural isomorphism \(\operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}\,A) \cong \operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}\,B)\)
  comes from a unique isomorphism \(A \cong B\) (via the preimage of the iso).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[A scheme isomorphism carries the corepresenting object along]
  \label{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_transport_along_iso}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso, lem:compCoyonedaIso_mathlib,
    lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes, \(V \subseteq X\) an open, and
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi : X.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq Y.\mathrm{Modules}\)
  the induced equivalence. Then the pushforward carries the corepresenting object of
  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou} along the isomorphism:
  \[
    \Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,V},
  \]
  where \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,V\) is the preimage of \(V\) under \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\), i.e.\
  the image open of \(V\) in \(Y\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso, lem:compCoyonedaIso_mathlib,
    lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib}
  The argument is a corepresentability transport; \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) is never unfolded. Write
  \(A = j_!\mathcal{O}_V\). It suffices, by full faithfulness of \(\operatorname{coyoneda}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib}, which reflects isomorphisms), to produce a natural
  isomorphism of the corepresented functors
  \(\operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A))) \cong
  \operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}))\); the desired
  isomorphism of objects is then its preimage.

  We build that natural isomorphism by chaining three identifications.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item The equivalence–coyoneda isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:compCoyonedaIso_mathlib}, applied to the
      adjunction underlying \(\Phi\), gives
      \(\operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A))) \cong
      \Phi.\mathrm{inverse} \circ \operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}\,A)\).
    \item The corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}, whiskered by the
      forgetful functor to types, identifies the corepresented functor with the sections functor:
      \(\operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}\,A) \cong \operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V\) (as
      \(\mathrm{Type}\)-valued functors). This rests on the corepresentability of \(\Gamma(V,-)\) by
      \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) of Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}.
    \item Sections of a pushforward are, by definition, sections over the preimage open:
      \(\Phi.\mathrm{inverse} \circ \operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V =
      \operatorname{sectionsFunctor}(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V)\), an equality of functors. Reading
      Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso} backwards at the open \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V\) then
      identifies this with \(\operatorname{coyoneda}(\mathrm{op}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}))\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Composing (1)–(3) yields the required natural isomorphism of corepresented functors, and
  Lemma~\ref{lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib} reflects it to the stated isomorphism of objects. No
  adjunction-mate transposition or naturality square is computed by hand: the only geometric inputs are
  the two definitional equalities ``corepresented functor \(=\) sections functor'' and ``sections of a
  pushforward \(=\) sections over the preimage''.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso preserves quasi-coherence]
  \label{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Section ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent
  % modules'', items (1),(7) and the closing remark on pushforward (read from
  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L4729--4767).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is
  % quasi-coherent if and only if the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$
  % to each affine open $U = \Spec(R)$ is of the form
  % $\widetilde M$ for some $R$-module $M$."
  % "Given a morphism of schemes $f : Y \to X$ the pullback
  % of a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is a quasi-coherent
  % $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module. See
  % Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}."
  % "On the other hand, it is in general not the case that the
  % pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.
  % Here is a case where this does hold."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent modules''.}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(H : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a
  quasi-coherent module. Then the pushforward \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) along
  \(\varphi\) is again quasi-coherent.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} property of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, checkable on any affine
  open cover: \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent iff its restriction to each affine open
  \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R\) is of the form \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(R\)-module \(M\) ---
  equivalently iff each member of an affine open covering carries a local presentation
  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_U \to 0\). This is the datum
  \(H\) carries as a quasi-coherent module (its \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\): an affine cover
  together with, on each member, the local presentation).

  The morphism \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of ringed spaces: its underlying map is a homeomorphism
  \(X \cong Y\) inducing an order-isomorphism of the open frames \(\operatorname{Opens} X \cong
  \operatorname{Opens} Y\), and it carries the structure-ring sheaf along, an isomorphism
  \(X.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf} \cong (\varphi)_*\, Y.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}\). Pushforward
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor} = \varphi_*\) along \(\varphi\) is therefore the \emph{transport} of module
  data across this isomorphism of ringed spaces: it relabels opens by the homeomorphism and twists the
  \(\mathcal{O}\)-action by the ring-sheaf isomorphism, with no further modification of the underlying
  abelian-sheaf data.

  Transport \(H\)'s quasi-coherent datum across \(\varphi\). Under the homeomorphism the affine open
  cover of \(X\) supporting \(H\) goes to an affine open cover of \(Y\) (the image of an affine open
  under a scheme isomorphism is affine, Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}). On each
  image member \(W = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,U\) the local presentation
  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to H|_U \to 0\) is carried by \(\varphi_*\) to a
  presentation \(\mathcal{O}_Y^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_Y^{(I)} \to (\varphi_* H)|_W \to 0\), because the
  ring-sheaf isomorphism sends finite free \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to finite free
  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules and transport, being an equivalence, is exact and so preserves the
  cokernel presentation. Hence \(\varphi_* H\) has a local presentation on an affine cover of \(Y\) and
  is quasi-coherent.

  Equivalently, since \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is the
  \emph{pullback} along the inverse isomorphism, and pullback of a quasi-coherent module is
  quasi-coherent (item (7) of the cited functoriality list, Modules
  Lemma~\texttt{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}); the by-hand presentation transport above is
  the unwinding of that fact in the present \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\) formalism.

  \emph{Why the transport is done by hand.} Mathlib's \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.pushforward}\)
  carries no general quasi-coherence-preservation lemma: a pushforward \(f_*\) preserves quasi-coherence
  only under extra hypotheses on \(f\) --- it is quasi-compact-quasi-separated, or an isomorphism. ``It
  is in general not the case that the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.'' The
  present statement is exactly the isomorphism case, which is not packaged as a reusable Mathlib lemma
  here; so the proof transports the local \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\) presentation across the
  isomorphism of ringed spaces explicitly, as above, rather than invoking a ready-made instance.
\end{proof}

  \label{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib}

  covering the terminal object. If each over-picture restriction \(\mathcal{F}.\operatorname{over} U_i\)
  is quasi-coherent, then \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Extraction of the local quasi-coherence datum]
  \label{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
  \lean{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent.nonempty_quasicoherentData}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  A quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{F}\) admits a quasi-coherence datum: there is a family

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso commutes with restriction to an open]
  \label{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_commutes_restriction}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes,
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi\) the induced equivalence of module categories,
  and \(V \subseteq X\) an open with image \(V' = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,V \subseteq Y\). The
  homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\) restricts to a homeomorphism \(V \cong V'\), hence to an
  equivalence of the opens-sites \(\operatorname{Opens} V \simeq \operatorname{Opens} V'\); let
  \[
    e : (X.\mathrm{Modules} \!\restriction\! V) \;\simeq\; (Y.\mathrm{Modules} \!\restriction\! V')
  \]
  be the induced equivalence of restricted-module categories obtained from the site-equivalence
  transport of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}. Then for every
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(H\) there is a comparison isomorphism
  \[
    e.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} V\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V',
  \]
  identifying ``restrict to \(V\) then push forward over \(V'\)'' with ``push forward then restrict to
  \(V'\)''.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
  Both sides are computed open-by-open on the subspace site of \(V'\). For an open
  \(W \subseteq V'\) with preimage \(W_0 = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}^{-1}\,W \subseteq V\), the sections of the
  right-hand restriction are the sections of \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) over the image
  open \(W\), which — pushforward acting on opens by the inverse-image homeomorphism — are the sections
  of \(H\) over \(W_0\) (the restriction-of-objects identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib},
  applied to the open immersions \(V \hookrightarrow X\) and \(V' \hookrightarrow Y\)). The same
  sections compute the left-hand side: the site-equivalence transport
  \(e\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib} relabels the open \(W\) of \(V'\)
  to the corresponding open \(W_0\) of \(V\) and reads off \(H.\operatorname{over} V\) there. The two
  presheaves of sections therefore agree, compatibly with restriction maps, and the comparison is a
  morphism of sheaves of modules over \(V'\); being an isomorphism on every section group it is an
  isomorphism. This is the open-restriction analogue of the \(\operatorname{bind}\) construction's
  per-piece transport, with the homeomorphism-induced opens-site equivalence in place of the
  iterated-slice equivalence.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso preserves quasi-coherence]
  \label{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction, lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib,
    lem:presentation_map_mathlib, lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib,
    lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib,
    lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Section ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent
  % modules'', items (1),(7) and the closing remark on pushforward (read from
  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L4729--4767).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is
  % quasi-coherent if and only if the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$
  % to each affine open $U = \Spec(R)$ is of the form
  % $\widetilde M$ for some $R$-module $M$."
  % "Given a morphism of schemes $f : Y \to X$ the pullback
  % of a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is a quasi-coherent
  % $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module. See
  % Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}."
  % "On the other hand, it is in general not the case that the
  % pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.
  % Here is a case where this does hold."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent modules''.}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(H : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a
  quasi-coherent module. Then the pushforward \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) along
  \(\varphi\) is again quasi-coherent.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction, lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib,
    lem:presentation_map_mathlib, lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib,
    lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib,
    lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} datum: it transports across the equivalence \(\Phi\) by moving
  \(H\)'s local presentations to the image cover, member by member. We never seek a global presentation
  of \(H\) (it has none — \(H\) is quasi-coherent only on the abstract source); we move the local
  presentations one open at a time.

  \emph{The cover.} Extract \(H\)'s quasi-coherence datum
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens covering
  \(X\) together with, for each \(i\), a presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\). Form the
  image cover \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) of \(Y\); it covers \(Y\) because the underlying
  map of \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\) is a homeomorphism, so the preimages of a cover form a cover (the image
  of an affine open under the scheme isomorphism is again affine,
  Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}, when one wants the cover to remain affine). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib} it suffices to show that
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) is quasi-coherent for every \(i\).

  \emph{Per-member transport.} Fix \(i\) and let
  \(e_i : (X.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! U_i) \simeq (Y.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! V_i)\) be
  the opens-site equivalence induced by \(\varphi\) restricted to \(U_i\), built from
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}. This \(e_i\) preserves colimits and carries
  the structure-sheaf unit to the structure-sheaf unit (it is a site equivalence whose underlying base
  is a homeomorphism), so by Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib} the presentation \(p_i\) of
  \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\) transports to a presentation of
  \(e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}(H.\operatorname{over} U_i)\). The comparison isomorphism of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction},
  \[
    e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V_i,
  \]
  then carries that presentation, via Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib}, to a presentation of
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\). A sheaf of modules with a (global)
  presentation is quasi-coherent, so each member of the image cover is quasi-coherent, and the claim
  follows by Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib}.

  This is the isomorphism case of the Stacks functoriality remark (``it is in general not the case that
  the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent''; here it holds because \(\varphi\) is an
  isomorphism, equivalently \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is a pullback). Mathlib packages no reusable
  quasi-coherence-preservation lemma for \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.pushforward}\), so the proof
  realises the transport explicitly as the per-member presentation move above, structurally mirroring the
  \(\operatorname{presentation}\) field of the quasi-coherent-data \(\operatorname{bind}\) construction.
\end{proof}
